text: This movie illustrates the surgical technique for robot-assisted minimally invasive partial resection of the spleen in a twenty-four-year-old female with a large post-traumatic splenic cyst. CAT scan reconstructions are extremely useful to pro-operatively assess size and location of the cyst and to precisely understand the vascular anatomy of the splenic hilum to plan the surgical strategy. Two ports are connected to the vincis operative arms, one to the robotic endoscope and another one remains available for surgical assistance.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colonic flexure by sectioning the gastrocolic ligament, entering the lesser sac. Short gastric vessels are coagulated with bipolar devices and sectioned using ultrasonic shears. The splenic artery is dissected, with a vascular loop placed around it to prevent direct manipulation. An endoscopic vascular clamp is applied to the splenic artery to minimize bleeding and reduce spleen volume.

The surgeon identifies and preserves two branches for the lower pole of the spleen while clipping the trunk vascularizing the upper pole. The vascular clamp is removed, and the main trunk of the splenic vein is dissected with a vascular loop placed for easier manipulation. The tail of the pancreas is detached from the splenic hilum, and the upper pole artery is clipped and transected. The superior branch of the splenic vein is clipped and sectioned, freeing the superior transected branches from the pancreatic tail. The demarcation line is identified, and the parenchymal transaction is completed using ultrasonic shears.

Bipolar forceps are used to achieve hemostasis on the transected splenic parenchyma. The surgeon sections the splenoprenic ligaments, completely freeing the transected spleen. Vascular loops are removed, and the spleen is placed in a bag for extraction through a small suprapubic incision. The splenoprenic ligament is reconstructed to prevent torsion of the remaining splenic tissue on its vascular pedicle.

A hemostatic matrix is applied to the dissected splenic parenchyma, and a small drainage tube is visibly left in place. The video concludes with the verification of regular vascularization of the residual spleen using echocolordoppler ultrasonography.
